era_context: The late 7th century CE is marked by the consolidation of the Umayyad Caliphate, the maturity of the Tang Dynasty in China, and the fragmentation of Merovingian power in Western Europe. The Byzantine Empire is under pressure from Arab expansion and internal religious disputes. This is a period of religious transformation, military consolidation, and the emergence of new political orders across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (no written sources for this specific year)
Oceania and Pacific islands (sparse archaeological dating)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited written sources)
Most of the Indian subcontinent (limited written sources for this specific year)

events.title: Umayyad military preparations and expansion
events.region: Umayyad Caliphate (Syria, Egypt, North Africa, Mesopotamia)
events.description: Muawiyah I (r. 661–680 CE) was consolidating Umayyad military power and preparing for major operations against the Byzantine Empire. In the early 670s, the Umayyads were building up their naval forces and conducting raids on Byzantine territories. These preparations would culminate in the First Arab Siege of Constantinople (674–678 CE). The Umayyad military machine was one of the most formidable forces in the Mediterranean world at this time.
key_figures: Umayyad military commanders (names vary in sources)

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Umayyad military preparations in the early 670s are attested in later sources, but specific events in 671 CE are not individually documented. This entry describes the broader context rather than a discrete event.
cross_references: Led to First Arab Siege of Constantinople (674–678 CE)

events.title: Tang Dynasty military campaigns in Central Asia
events.description: The Tang Dynasty under Emperor Gaozong (r. 649–683 CE) was engaged in military campaigns to expand and consolidate Tang control over Central Asia. These campaigns were directed against the Eastern Turks and other rivals for control of the Silk Road trade routes. The Tang military was one of the most formidable forces in Asia at this time, and these campaigns represented the height of Tang military expansion. The conquest and control of Central Asia was crucial to Tang economic power and cultural influence.
key_figures: Wu Zetian (empress consort, de facto power)
Tang military commanders (names vary in sources)
events.certainty_note: Tang military campaigns in Central Asia in the 670s are attested in later sources, but specific events in 671 CE are not individually documented. This entry describes the broader context of Tang expansion.
cross_references: Part of broader Tang Dynasty expansion into Central Asia (618–907 CE)

events.title: King Ecgfrith of Northumbria consolidates power
events.description: King Ecgfrith of Northumbria (r. 670–685 CE) was consolidating his power in the early years of his reign. Ecgfrith was one of the most powerful Anglo-Saxon kings of his time, and his reign saw the expansion of Northumbrian power and influence. He was engaged in military campaigns against the Picts, the kingdom of Strathclyde, and other rivals. His reign represented the height of Northumbrian power, though it would end in defeat and his death at the Battle of Nechtansmere in 685 CE.
key_figures: Northumbrian nobles and military commanders (names vary in sources)

sources.name: Bede, Ecclesiastical History of the English People

events.certainty_note: Ecgfrith's reign and consolidation of power are well-attested in Anglo-Saxon sources. Specific events in 671 CE are not individually documented; this entry describes the broader context of his reign.
cross_references: Led to Battle of Nechtansmere in 685 CE, which ended Northumbrian expansion

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events in 671 CE are well-attested in contemporary sources. All entries describe broader historical contexts rather than discrete events. The dating of Umayyad military preparations, Tang military campaigns, and Northumbrian consolidation to 671 CE is conventional rather than based on contemporary evidence. Modern scholarship disputes the exact timeline of various military and political developments; some sources suggest different dates or interpretations.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 671 CE is very sparse. No contemporary sources directly document events in this specific year. All information is derived from later compilations written 100–300 years after the events, or from broader historical contexts inferred from archaeological evidence or later chronicles. Byzantine sources are fragmentary. Islamic sources are abundant but written from a later perspective. Anglo-Saxon sources are limited and often contradictory. The year 671 CE itself is not a natural boundary in any major historical tradition; events are assigned to this year by modern convention. Significant bias toward Mediterranean and European history in surviving sources; East Asian sources are more abundant but often court-sponsored.

graph_edges.note: Umayyad military preparations in 671 CE were part of the buildup to the siege

graph_edges.note: Tang military campaigns in 671 CE were part of broader expansion
